_doc: Agent-authored NO-APPEAL judgment for 530 Desnoyer Ave, St. Paul. The scenario (fairly_assessed) and the $0 reduction are DERIVED by scripts.build_finding, not asserted here — there are no central extraction comps below EMV, so the builder concludes the value AT the current EMV. The `finding` block is the agent's narrative (rendered verbatim). Illustrative worked example. Render: uv run python -m scripts.build_finding properties/desnoyer/judgment.json --output properties/desnoyer/finding.html

meta.report_title: Assessment Review Findings — 530 Desnoyer Ave, St. Paul
meta.banner: ILLUSTRATIVE SAMPLE — built from public Ramsey County assessment and sale data. Not a filed document.

finding.summary_headline: No clear angle — the assessment is supported by recent sales.
finding.summary_body: We pulled the county data and the recent neighborhood sales. The subject's building value per square foot looks high against a broad neighborhood median, which is worth a look — but against its immediate Otis Ave / Pelham peers it is mid-pack, and a recent arm's-length sale two blocks away supports values at this level. The county also already trimmed the 2026 EMV 3.2%. We don't see a clear angle to appeal.
work_completed: Pulled the 3-year assessment history from Ramsey County
Compared the subject's land and building $/SF to same-size peers
Identified the strongest comp the county would lead with
Tested the building $/SF signal against actual market sales

findings.title: The county already cut the 2026 value 3.2%
findings.body: After a 12.8% jump in 2025 ($765,600 → $863,800), the county trimmed the 2026 EMV to $836,500 (−$27,300, −3.2%). The aggressive year was already partly corrected.

findings.title: Building $/SF looks high against a broad median — worth checking
findings.body: The subject's building is assessed at $298/SF vs. a $218 same-size neighborhood median. On its own that reads as a possible over-assessment, so we tested it against the market.

findings.title: ...but against the immediate peer group it is mid-pack
findings.body: The closest same-street peers run higher, not lower: 472 Otis $316/SF, 375 Pelham $306/SF, 492 Otis $272/SF. The subject's $298/SF sits inside that immediate range. The broad median is pulled down by lower-value pockets that aren't truly comparable.

findings.title: A recent arm's-length sale supports the value
findings.body: 38 Otis Ave (2,170 SF, two blocks away) sold for $800,000 in April 2025 and is itself assessed at $765,800. The subject is larger (2,369 SF); its $836,500 EMV is consistent with that sale on a per-SF basis.

findings.title: Neighborhood assessments run slightly below market
findings.body: Across 56 arm's-length sales the median EMV-to-sale ratio is 0.976 — assessments run about 2.4% below sale prices. The subject is not an outlier the other way.

bldg_psf_chart.caption: Building $/SF vs. finished SF — subject (highlighted) sits inside the immediate Otis/Pelham peer range, not above it.

killer_comp.summary: The strongest comp the county would lead with: a recent arm's-length sale two blocks away, similar era, slightly smaller.
killer_comp.matters_body: A real, recent, arm's-length sale at this value level — assessed below its own sale price — is the evidence an appeal would have to overcome. It does not support a reduction.

final_bullets: The 2026 EMV of $836,500 is supported by a recent arm's-length sale two blocks away.
The building $/SF signal does not survive contact with the immediate peer group or the market.
The county already corrected most of the 2025 jump (−3.2% for 2026).
If filed, the county would lead with the 38 Otis sale, and we'd have no clear answer.
